sections.title: ال — The One and Only Article
sections.explanation: Arabic has only one article: ال (al-), which is the definite article meaning 'the'. There is no indefinite article in Arabic — a noun without ال is automatically indefinite. The article ال is always attached directly to the beginning of the noun as a prefix. For example, كتاب means 'a book', and الكتاب means 'the book'. The article never changes for gender, number, or case — it is always ال regardless of whether the noun is masculine, feminine, singular, or plural. In formal Arabic, an indefinite noun takes the suffix -n (called tanwīn or nunation): كتابٌ (kitābun, 'a book') vs. الكتابُ (al-kitābu, 'the book').
sections.tip: Unlike English which has 'a/an' and 'the', Arabic only has 'the' (ال). If there is no ال, the noun is automatically indefinite. This makes the system simpler once you get used to it.

examples.en: The boy is in the house — both nouns have ال

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'the book' in Arabic?
exercises.explanation: Adding the prefix ال (al-) to كتاب (book) makes it الكتاب (the book). The article ال always attaches directly to the front of the noun.

exercises.prompt: What does a noun WITHOUT ال indicate in Arabic?
exercises.explanation: Arabic has no word for 'a' or 'an'. A noun without ال is automatically indefinite. So كتاب by itself means 'a book'.

exercises.prompt: Does the article ال change based on the gender or number of the noun?
options: Yes, it becomes الة for feminine nouns
Yes, it doubles for plural nouns
exercises.explanation: The definite article ال never changes. It is always ال regardless of whether the noun is masculine, feminine, singular, dual, or plural.

sections.title: Sun Letters (الحروف الشمسية)
sections.explanation: Arabic letters are divided into two groups based on how they interact with the definite article ال: sun letters and moon letters. There are 14 sun letters: ت ث د ذ ر ز س ش ص ض ط ظ ل ن. When ال precedes a sun letter, the ل (lām) of ال is silent — instead, the sun letter is doubled (geminated). This is called assimilation: the 'l' sound merges into the following consonant. For example, الشمس (the sun) is pronounced 'ash-shams', not 'al-shams'. The ل is still written but not pronounced. In formal writing, the doubling is shown with a شدّة (shadda) mark: الشَّمس. Sun letters are all articulated with the tongue near the teeth or the front of the palate — close to where 'l' is produced — which is why the assimilation occurs.
sections.tip: The word for 'sun' is شمس — and ش is a sun letter. Say 'ash-shams' (not 'al-shams'). A quick test: if saying 'al-' before the letter feels awkward and your tongue naturally doubles the next sound, it is a sun letter.

examples.en: the sun — pronounced 'ash-shams', not 'al-shams'; ش is a sun letter
the people — pronounced 'an-nās', not 'al-nās'; ن is a sun letter
the lesson — pronounced 'ad-dars', not 'al-dars'; د is a sun letter
the peace — pronounced 'as-salām', not 'al-salām'; س is a sun letter

exercises.prompt: How is الشمس (the sun) correctly pronounced?
exercises.explanation: The letter ش (shīn) is a sun letter, so the ل of ال assimilates into it. The result is a doubled ش: ash-shams. The ل is written but not pronounced.

exercises.prompt: What happens when ال comes before a sun letter?
options: The ل is silent and the sun letter is doubled
Nothing special — it is pronounced 'al' as usual
exercises.explanation: Before a sun letter, the ل (lām) of the article becomes silent and the following sun letter is doubled (geminated). This assimilation happens because sun letters are articulated near the same place as 'l'.

exercises.prompt: How many sun letters are there in Arabic?
exercises.explanation: There are 14 sun letters (ت ث د ذ ر ز س ش ص ض ط ظ ل ن) and 14 moon letters. Together they cover all 28 letters of the Arabic alphabet.

sections.title: Moon Letters (الحروف القمرية)
sections.explanation: The remaining 14 letters are moon letters: ا ب ج ح خ ع غ ف ق ك م ه و ي. When ال precedes a moon letter, the ل is fully pronounced as 'l'. For example, القمر (the moon) is pronounced 'al-qamar' — you clearly hear the 'l'. The name 'moon letters' comes from the word قمر (moon), where ق is a moon letter, and 'sun letters' from شمس (sun), where ش is a sun letter. Moon letters are generally articulated further from the tongue tip — at the lips, back of the throat, or with other articulators — so they do not naturally assimilate with 'l'. Recognizing whether a letter is a sun or moon letter will help you read aloud correctly and understand native speakers better.
sections.tip: If you can comfortably say 'al-' before the letter without your tongue wanting to skip the 'l', it is a moon letter. The word القمر (al-qamar, the moon) is the model — you clearly hear 'al-'.

examples.en: the moon — pronounced 'al-qamar'; ق is a moon letter, so the ل is heard
the book — pronounced 'al-kitāb'; ك is a moon letter
the house — pronounced 'al-bayt'; ب is a moon letter
the boy — pronounced 'al-walad'; و is a moon letter

exercises.explanation: The letter ق (qāf) is a moon letter, so the ل of the article is fully pronounced. You say 'al-qamar', clearly hearing the 'l' sound.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a moon letter?
exercises.explanation: The letter ب (bāʼ) is a moon letter — it is articulated at the lips, far from where 'l' is produced. The letters ت and ن are sun letters because they are articulated near the teeth, close to where 'l' is made.

exercises.prompt: In الكتاب (the book), is the ل of ال pronounced?
options: Yes, because ك is a moon letter
exercises.explanation: The letter ك (kāf) is a moon letter, so the ل of ال is clearly pronounced: 'al-kitāb'. If the next letter were a sun letter (like ش), the ل would be silent.
